info.title: Craftboxx API Documentation
info.description:
This document provides a comprehensive guide to integrating with the Craftboxx API. It is designed for developers looking to build applications and services that interact with the Craftboxx platform.

### Authentication

To use the API, you must first generate a Bearer access token.

1. Obtain a Token: Send a `POST` request with your employee credentials (email and password) to the `/auth/create-token` endpoint.
2. Authorize Requests: Include the obtained token in the `Authorization` header for every subsequent API call. The header should be formatted as follows:
   `Authorization: Bearer <YOUR_ACCESS_TOKEN>`

Your API access is governed by the same permissions you have in the Craftboxx Planner. If your token is invalid or expired, the API will return a `401 Unauthorized` status code.

### API Principles

This API largely follows RESTful design principles. Endpoints are organized around resources, and it uses standard HTTP methods for operations.

### Requests for Resource Collections

When fetching a list of resources (e.g., `/articles`, `/projects`), you can use a variety of query parameters to refine the results.

#### Filtering and Searching

- Generic Search (`q`): Performs a broad search across all relevant fields of a resource.
  - Example: `/articles?q=Bosch Akkuschrauber`
- Filter by ID (`id[]`): Retrieves specific resources by their unique IDs.
  - Example (Single): `/articles?id=50`
  - Example (Multiple): `/articles?id[]=50&id[]=51`
- Exclude by ID (`exclude[]`): Excludes specific resources from the results.
  - Example (Single): `/articles?exclude=50`
  - Example (Multiple): `/articles?exclude[]=50&exclude[]=51`
- Attribute Filtering: Filters results based on specific attribute values. This supports exact matches, SQL-like `LIKE` searches with the `%` wildcard, and multiple values using array syntax.
  - Example (LIKE search): `/articles?manufacturer=Bosch%`
  - Example (Multiple values): `/articles?manufacturer[]=Bosch&manufacturer[]=Makita`
- Comparison Operators: For more precise filtering on attributes like dates or numbers, you can specify a comparison operator.
  - Example (Date greater than or equal to): `date_comparison=%3E%3D&date=2025-05-01` (where `%3E%3D` is the URL-encoded version of `>=`)
  - Allowed Operators: `LIKE`, `NOT LIKE`, `=`, `!=`, `>`, `>=`, `<`, `<=`
- Date Formats: While many date formats are accepted, the recommended format is ISO-8601 with a timezone, such as `2025-01-01T12:00:00+01:00`.

#### Sorting

- Sort Order (`order_by` & `order_direction`): Sorts the result set by a specific attribute in either ascending or descending order.
  - Example: `/articles?order_by=manufacturer&order_direction=desc`

#### Pagination

- Paginating Results (`per_page` & `page`): Controls the number of items per page and which page of results to return.
  - Example: `/articles?per_page=25&page=3`

#### Working with Relationships

- Load Relations (`with`): Eager-loads related resources to include them in the response, reducing the need for subsequent API calls.
  - Example (Single): `/articles?with=assignments`
  - Example (Multiple): `/employees?with[]=assignments&with[]=projects`
- Filter Fields (`only`): Returns only the specified attributes of a resource, supporting nested structures with dot notation.
   - Example (Single): `/articles?only=id`
   - Example (Multiple): `/articles?only[]=id&only[]=name`
   - Example (Nested): `/assignments?with=project&only[]=id&only[]=project.id`
- Filter by Relations: Retrieves resources that are associated with another specific resource.
   - Example (Get all articles for assignment 50): `/articles?assignments=50`
- Count Relations (`count`): Returns a count of related resources instead of the resources themselves.
   - Example (Single): `/assignments?with_count=articles`
   - Example (Multiple): `/assignments?with_count[]=articles&with_count[]=employees`
- Min / Max value of a Relation: Retrieves the minimum or maximum value of a relation.
   - Example (Single): `/projects?with_min=assignment.start&with_max=assignment.end`
   - Example (Multiple): `/projects?with_min[]=assignment.start&with_min[]=documentations.documented_on&with_max[]=assignment.end&with_max[]=documentations.documented_on`
- Sum of a Relation: Retrieves the sum of a relation.
   - Example (Single): `/assignment?with_sum=billable_timesheet_activites.duration_seconds`
   - Example (Multiple): `/assignment?with_sum[]=billable_timesheet_activites.duration_seconds`
- Filter by Relational Properties: Filters based on the properties of a related model. The model and property are separated by a hyphen.
   - Example (Find projects by a customer's customer number): `/projects?customer-customer_number=123`

Note: For a complete list of filterable attributes for a resource, refer to the attributes available in its corresponding `POST` or `PUT` request body.

### Requests for a Single Resource

To retrieve a single resource, make a `GET` request to the resource endpoint with its unique ID.

- Example (Single): `/articles/50`
- Filter Fields (`only`): Returns only the specified attributes of a resource (Same as the `only` parameter for resource collections)
    - Example (Single): `/articles/50?only=id`
    - Example (Multiple): `/articles/50?only[]=id&only[]=name`
- Load Relations (`load`): You can use `load` for eager-loading related resources (Similar to the `with` parameter for resource collections)
    - Example (Single): `/timesheets/123?load[]=employee`
    - Example (Multiple): `/timesheets/123?load[]=activities&load[]=employee`
- If a resource does not exist, the API will return a `404 Not Found` error.

### HTTP Methods Overview

| Method   | Action                                      | Successful Response | Error Responses                                |
| :------- | :------------------------------------------ | :------------------ | :--------------------------------------------- |
| GET      | Retrieves one or a collection of resources. | `200 OK`            | `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found`            |
| POST     | Creates a new resource.                     | `201 Created`       | `401 Unauthorized`, `422 Unprocessable Entity` |
| PUT      | Updates an existing resource.               | `200 OK`            | `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found`, `422 Unprocessable Entity` |
| DELETE   | Deletes a resource.                         | `200 OK`            | `401 Unauthorized`, `404 Not Found`            |

### Common Status Codes

| Code | Meaning                  | Description                                                                 |
| :--- | :----------------------- | :-------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| 200  | OK                       | The request was successful.                                                 |
| 201  | Created                  | The resource was successfully created (used for `POST` requests).           |
| 401  | Unauthorized             | The request lacks valid authentication credentials.                         |
| 404  | Not Found                | The requested resource could not be found.                                  |
| 422  | Unprocessable Entity     | The request was well-formed, but contains semantic errors (e.g., validation failed). |
| 500  | Internal Server Error    | An unexpected error occurred on the server, often when a requested relation does not exist. |

parameters.description: The appointment ids to share. Deliver none to share the whole project

post.summary: Adds timesheet activity type to a timesheet
Adds timesheet activity to a timesheet
Adds a break in the middle of the timesheet depending on the company settings.

204.description: Will be returned if the break can not be created. Reasons can be: There is already one break tracked, not enough working time or the auto break feature not activated.

adjust_blocking.description: Sometimes activities before or after would block this new activity. Set this parameter to true, to shorten or delete the blocking activities.

end_open_activities.description: Close possible activities with end = null by the logged in user as well.

absence--employees.name: employees[]
absence--employees.description: Employees Ids. Multiple input with employees[]=3&employees[]=6

absence--types.name: types[]
absence--types.description: Absence types ids. Multiple input with types[]=3&types[]=6

general--exclude.name: exclude[]
general--exclude.description: Exclude ids. Multiple input with exclude[]=3&exclude[]=6

general--id.name: id[]
general--id.description: Filters by ids. Multiple input with ids[]=3&ids[]=6

general--with.name: with[]
general--with.description: You can load relations using the with parameter. Example for assignments/worksehets:  with[]=documents loads all related documents and adds it to the response.

general--order_direction.name: order_direction
general--order_direction.description: Order direction. asc or desc possible

schedulable--started.name: started
schedulable--started.description: Same as start parameter, but its using overlapping logic. Useful when you want to see for example all appointments active on these day. Example: Models has 'start' = 2025-01-01, 'end' = 2025-01-03. Parameter 'started' = 2025-01-02 would include this, as its period is overlapping with 'started' parameter

schedulable--ended.name: ended
schedulable--ended.description: Similar as started, but vice versa

schedulable--all_day.name: all_day
schedulable--all_day.description: Is it all day or not?
